assets.name: lecture_01_figure_02.png
assets.rationale: This frame gives the clearest overall board layout for the moment: both regime labels are legible, the left-hand multi-arrow sketch is reasonably exposed, and the right-hand vertical sketch is still visible. The lecturer occludes less of the useful chalk than in the neighboring frames, and the board state looks finished enough to support a cautious redraw for the notes.
assets.subtitle_excerpt: That energy is a kind of potential energy.

assets.name: lecture_01_figure_03.png
assets.rationale: This is the most finished frame in the window. It captures the full intent of the asymmetrical drawing: the left beveled end, the long uneven body, and the rounded right end are all visible. Although the lecturer blocks part of the middle, the overall shape is more complete and more useful for a later redraw than the earlier, still-developing frames.
assets.subtitle_excerpt: I'm going to draw it asymmetrically

assets.name: lecture_01_figure_04.png
assets.rationale: This frame best preserves the geometrical board picture at the moment Susskind explicitly calls it a geometrical picture. The left-hand cylindrical shape is clearly visible, the long upper contour and the slanted rightward extension are both present, and the board state looks more complete than in candidate 1 while being slightly less cluttered than the neighboring frames.
assets.subtitle_excerpt: This is, of course, a geometrical picture.

assets.name: lecture_01_figure_06.png
assets.rationale: This is the clearest frame in the window for the standing board layout. The cylinder-like sketch on the left and the long horizontal guide lines above it are both visible with minimal motion blur, and the lecturer's posture does not hide much more of the drawing than in the neighboring frames. It is the best reference here if the notes need a screenshot-backed redraw of the geometry while the discussion turns to specially symmetric points.
assets.subtitle_excerpt: points. And there are specially symmetric
